title: Financial Instruments
description:
Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS 109 vs IFRS 9 vs US GAAP ASC
            815/326

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Financial instruments are less comprehensively covered; classification and measurement are simpler and less rule-rich.
Financial instruments are based on contractual rights and obligations, with classification driven by business model and cash flow characteristics.
Financial instruments are covered by detailed recognition, measurement, and disclosure guidance.
From simplicity to principle-based, risk-sensitive accounting.
Includes instruments creating financial asset, liability, or equity relationships.
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another.
Same general concept, but applied through US GAAP codification.
Recognize when the entity becomes party to the contractual provisions.
Recognize when control/rights and obligations arise under applicable guidance.
Usually at cost or fair value depending on guidance.
Generally fair value + transaction costs (except FVPL items).
Generally fair value + transaction costs (except for trading items).
Fair value becomes the starting point.
Amortised cost, FVOCI, or FVTPL depending on business model and SPPI test.
Category-based model (e.g., HTM, trading, AFS) - different buckets from IFRS.
Business model + cash flow characteristics drive classification.
Amortised cost, except liabilities at FVTPL.
Broadly similar intent, but detailed rules and presentation differ.
Generally measured at fair value through P&L unless hedge accounting applies.
Generally measured at fair value through earnings, with hedge accounting under ASC 815.
Derivatives always on balance sheet at fair value.
Limited or less codified; often incurred loss.
Expected credit loss (ECL) model for applicable assets and loan commitments.
Expected credit loss model (CECL under ASC 326) - different mechanics.
Derecognize when contractual cash flow rights expire or are transferred and risks/ rewards are considered.
Similar concept, but detailed tests differ.
Formal hedge accounting model with qualifying criteria and documentation (IFRS 9).
Hedge accounting exists but under different rules (ASC 815) with more bright lines.
Economic hedging can reduce P&L volatility if documented.
Offset only when legally enforceable right and intention to settle net exist.
Similar principle, but detailed requirements differ.

Bond: Recognised at 100 Cr. Interest expense accrued at 8% = 8 Cr per year. No fair value adjustment. Trade receivable: Recognised at 50 Cr. No ECL provision - only actual write-offs when known. Interest rate swap: Not recognised on balance sheet. No fair value change in P&L. Year 1 P&L: Interest expense 8 Cr. No derivative loss. No credit loss expense. Total financial expense = 8 Cr. Year 1 Balance Sheet: Bond liability 100 Cr. Receivable 50 Cr. No derivative asset/liability.

Bond: Amortised cost - 100 Cr principal, interest expense = 8 Cr. Trade receivable: Amortised cost with ECL. Expected credit loss allowance = 2% × 50 Cr = 1 Cr. Net receivable = 49 Cr. Interest rate swap: Recognised at FVTPL. Fair value loss 2 Cr recognised in P&L. Swap liability of 2 Cr on BS. Year 1 P&L: Interest 8 Cr + derivative loss 2 Cr + credit loss expense 1 Cr = 11 Cr total financial expense.

Bond: Amortised cost - 100 Cr principal, interest expense = 8 Cr. Trade receivable: Amortised cost with ECL. Expected credit loss allowance = 2% × 50 Cr = 1 Cr. Net receivable = 49 Cr. Interest rate swap: Recognised at FVTPL. Fair value loss 2 Cr recognised in P&L. Swap liability of 2 Cr on BS. Year 1 P&L: Interest 8 Cr + derivative loss 2 Cr + credit loss expense 1 Cr = 11 Cr total financial expense.

Bond: Amortisation cost (8 Cr interest). Trade receivable: CECL allowance of 1 Cr. Interest rate swap: FVTPL with fair value loss of 2 Cr through earnings. Year 1 P&L: Total financial expense matches Ind AS/IFRS: 11 Cr.

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - Year 1

tableData: Assuming Ind/IFRS policy choice to classify interest paid as financing
Interest paid shifted to financing under Ind AS/IFRS policy
Total cash drain is identical across all frameworks

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Fair value changes flow through P&L (no hedge accounting)
Earnings are sensitive to market rates
Separate operating results from market remeasurement
Review ageing, macro assumptions, and concentration
Bond at amortised cost - no fair value volatility
Inspect policy choices and business model
Swap loss 2 Cr not core operating
Include derivative liabilities and ECL allowances
Leverage is more truthful, not necessarily worse
Separate derivative fair value changes from core interest
Real coverage may be higher if swap is economic hedge
Add back ECL and derivative losses to profit, adjust assets
Operating returns are higher than reported
Compare on adjusted earnings (exclude non-operating FV changes)
Volatility reflects accounting, not business deterioration
Strip out non-operating fair value effects
Underlying EPS closer to AS but with credit cost
No, risk was already there (derivative, credit). Now it's visible
Swap liability 2 Cr and ECL allowance recognised
Why is PAT lower under new standards?
Because derivative losses and expected credit losses are now expensed, not hidden
11 Cr total expense vs 8 Cr under AS
Are IFRS and US GAAP comparable for derivatives?
Broadly yes for fair value, but hedge accounting mechanics differ
Both recognise 2 Cr loss; US GAAP has more bright lines
Is the swap liability equal to debt?
Economically similar to a financing obligation but not contractually debt
Fair value of derivative is 2 Cr liability
Normalise earnings by removing market-driven derivative P&L, add ECL to receivables
Use adjusted EBIT and adjusted debt
Recognise that derivative loss and ECL are non-cash or expected, not realised
Higher liabilities (102 Cr) and lower ROE
Rebuild ROIC with adjusted debt and normalised earnings
Better transparency of derivative and credit risk
Mixed - likes disclosure but dislikes volatility
Focus on hedge strategy and credit quality
Bond at amortised cost hides no volatility; derivative volatility may be elsewhere
May be hiding aggressive credit loss estimates
Explains swap fair value loss in detail
Management understands market risk and communicates it
Presents adjusted earnings excluding derivative swings
Wants to show core operating performance
Frequent redesignation of instruments (e.g., FVOCI vs FVTPL)
Review business model rationale each period

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Large derivative liabilities with weak disclosure
Rising ECL allowance but stable macro
Derivative liability rising faster than notional
Request scenario stress test on margin calls.
Clear ECL methodology and sensitivity analysis
Rely on reported figures for true economic cost.
Index-linked or inflation-linked derivatives clearly explained
Treat as genuine prudent hedging rather than speculation.
